content.text: Representational State Transfer (REST) is an architectural style for distributed hypermedia systems, defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It describes a set of six architectural constraints that, when applied to a network API, produce a system with desirable properties: scalability, simplicity, modifiability, visibility, portability, and reliability.
REST is not a protocol or a standard — it is an
: a set of constraints that shape how components in a distributed system interact. An API that satisfies all the constraints is said to be

The six constraints defined by Fielding are: client-server separation, statelessness, cacheability, a layered system, an optional code-on-demand constraint, and a uniform interface. The uniform interface itself is decomposed into four sub-constraints: identification of resources, manipulation of resources through representations, self-descriptive messages, and hypermedia as the engine of application state (HATEOAS).

In practice, most modern "REST APIs" implement a subset of these constraints — typically the first four and the identification/manipulation sub-constraints of the uniform interface — and omit HATEOAS. True HATEOAS compliance is rare in the industry but the other constraints are widely followed and produce meaningful benefits.

Before REST, most distributed systems used Remote Procedure Call (RPC) protocols like CORBA, SOAP, or DCOM. These protocols tied clients tightly to server implementations, made versioning difficult, and generated enormous interoperability problems. A client written for one SOAP service could not easily communicate with a different service without custom integration work.
REST addressed this by centering the interface on

rather than procedures. Any client that speaks HTTP and understands JSON can call a RESTful API without prior knowledge of the server's implementation. This openness enabled the explosion of third-party integrations and the "API economy" of the 2010s.

For agentic AI systems, REST principles matter because tool-calling agents interact with APIs. Well-designed RESTful APIs are self-describing (their URLs reveal the structure of the resources) and predictable (HTTP verbs have defined semantics), making it easier to generate correct tool calls and to reason about API responses.
Think of a REST API as a digital filing cabinet. Each drawer is a resource collection (
). Each folder in a drawer is an individual resource (

). You interact with the cabinet using a fixed set of standard operations: get a folder (GET), add a folder (POST/PUT), update a folder's contents (PUT/PATCH), or remove a folder (DELETE). You never call a custom "fetchUserWithOrders" procedure — you navigate the cabinet's structure with the standard operations.

The stateless constraint adds another dimension: the cabinet does not remember who you are between visits. Every time you interact, you bring your identity card (the Authorization header) with you. The cabinet checks the card, fulfils the request, and forgets. This makes the cabinet scale easily — any copy of the cabinet can handle any request.
— statelessness means any server replica can handle any request. Load balancers can distribute requests freely without sticky sessions.
— GET responses can be cached by HTTP infrastructure (browsers, CDNs, reverse proxies) without API-specific logic.
— any HTTP client in any language can call a REST API. No shared libraries or code generation required.
— HTTP verbs and status codes provide semantic meaning that monitoring infrastructure, proxies, and developers understand without documentation.
— the client-server separation means the server can change its implementation freely as long as the resource contracts are preserved.
— mapping domain objects to resources is a natural modelling exercise. Most developers understand CRUD operations on resources intuitively.
— fetching a user with their orders and each order's items may require multiple round trips. GraphQL or a purpose-built endpoint handles this more efficiently.

— a fixed GET /users/{id} response always returns all user fields even when the client only needs the email. GraphQL solves this; REST requires additional query parameter conventions or bespoke endpoints.
— unlike gRPC with Protocol Buffers, REST JSON payloads have no enforced schema at the transport level. OpenAPI partially compensates, but schema validation is optional.
— the most ambitious constraint is almost universally skipped, meaning REST APIs in practice are not as self-describing as Fielding's original vision.
— for high-throughput internal services, HTTP's header overhead and text encoding are costlier than binary protocols like gRPC.
A REST resource is any concept that can be named and addressed. Resources are identified by URLs. URL design conventions:
for orders belonging to a user
Use query parameters for filtering and sorting:
The HTTP specification assigns semantics to methods that REST reuses. An API should respect these semantics:
— retrieve a representation of a resource. Safe (no side effects) and idempotent.
— create a new resource. Not idempotent — calling twice creates two resources.
— replace a resource entirely. Idempotent — calling twice has the same effect as calling once.
— partially update a resource. Semantics depend on implementation.
HTTP status codes communicate the outcome of a request. REST APIs should use them precisely:
— successful GET, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE
— resource created by POST; include Location header
— successful action with no response body
— client error; malformed request or validation failure
— request is well-formed but semantically invalid
The diagram below shows a REST request-response cycle and the six constraints that govern it.

The client and server are separated — neither knows about the other's internal implementation. The server is stateless: every request contains all the information needed to process it. Responses can be cached by HTTP infrastructure between client and server. The system may be layered (load balancers, caches, API gateways) without the client or server's knowledge.
REST is a good fit when:
Building a public API intended to be consumed by multiple clients (browser, mobile, third-party).
The domain maps naturally to resources with CRUD operations.
HTTP caching provides meaningful performance benefits (read-heavy content APIs).
Interoperability across languages and platforms is a requirement.
A real-time bidirectional channel is needed — use WebSockets or SSE.
High-frequency internal service communication requires minimal overhead — use gRPC.
Clients need flexible queries over a complex graph of related data — use GraphQL.

# URL versioning (most common approach)
# v1 router
from fastapi import APIRouter
v1 = APIRouter(prefix="/v1")

@v1.get("/users/{user_id}")
def get_user_v1(user_id: int):
    ...  # original schema

# v2 router — adds 'metadata' field
v2 = APIRouter(prefix="/v2")

@v2.get("/users/{user_id}")
def get_user_v2(user_id: int):
    ...  # extended schema

app.include_router(v1)
app.include_router(v2)
Stripe's API is widely cited as a well-designed RESTful API. Resources are clearly named (
). HTTP verbs are used precisely. Error responses follow a consistent schema with
fields. Idempotency is supported via an
header on POST requests, allowing safe retries of payment operations.
Stripe's API versioning uses dated version strings (e.g.,
) rather than URL prefixes, demonstrating that versioning is a design choice with trade-offs rather than a single prescribed approach.
URLs should identify resources, not actions.
is an RPC-style path, not a REST resource path. The correct form is
— the HTTP method carries the action.
for every response, including errors, breaks HTTP clients' ability to interpret the response without parsing the body. This also breaks caching, monitoring, and retry logic.
) leak information about the size of the user base and enable enumeration attacks. UUIDs or opaque tokens are more secure for sensitive resources.
— generates interactive documentation, client libraries, and validation middleware automatically.
— use a standard error schema (e.g., RFC 9457 Problem Details) across all endpoints so clients can handle errors generically.
on day one is much cheaper than versioning a mature API later.
— allow clients to request specific fields via
— unbounded list responses are a denial-of-service vulnerability and break clients with large datasets.
for sensitive resources; appropriate max-age for stable content.
REST's statelessness is its primary scalability mechanism. Because the server holds no client session state, any server replica can handle any request. Horizontal scaling is trivial.

HTTP caching provides a second scalability lever. GET responses marked cacheable are served by CDN edges and browser caches without reaching the origin server. A well-designed REST API for read-heavy content (product catalogs, documentation) can serve the vast majority of requests from cache.

For write-heavy workloads or real-time requirements, REST over HTTP/2 improves on HTTP/1.1 by multiplexing requests over a single connection and compressing headers. HTTP/3 (QUIC) further reduces latency on lossy networks.
The standard for REST API documentation. FastAPI generates OpenAPI specs automatically from Python type annotations. Swagger UI renders the spec as interactive documentation.
Command-line HTTP clients for manual API testing. HTTPie provides a friendlier syntax:
GUI tools for constructing, saving, and organising API requests. Support environment variables for base URLs and authentication tokens.
Consumer-driven contract testing tool. Ensures that a REST client's assumptions about a server's API are verified against the server, preventing integration failures when the server changes.
REST APIs should be secured with HTTPS on every endpoint — HTTP leaks credentials and payloads. Authentication is most commonly handled via
headers. API keys are used for server-to-server integration.

Idempotency keys on non-idempotent operations (POST, PATCH) allow clients to safely retry requests after network failures without duplicate side effects. The server stores a record of processed idempotency keys and returns the cached response for duplicates.
Rate limiting protects against abuse. The server should return
header when the limit is exceeded. CORS headers must be configured carefully to prevent cross-origin data exfiltration.
REST is an architectural style defined by six constraints, not a protocol — the most important are statelessness, uniform interface, and client-server separation.
Resources are identified by URLs using nouns; HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) express actions on those resources.
HTTP status codes carry semantic meaning that clients, proxies, caches, and monitoring systems depend on — use them precisely.
Statelessness enables horizontal scaling: any server replica can handle any request with no session affinity required.
Publish an OpenAPI specification from day one to enable documentation, client generation, and validation.
Use versioned URL prefixes (/v1/) to allow the API to evolve without breaking existing clients.
Secure REST APIs with HTTPS, Bearer tokens, rate limiting, and idempotency keys on non-idempotent operations.
